perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall endeavor to restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is deemed necessary and shall encourage those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall carefully restrict' with 'shall endeavor to restrict' and 'as is reasonably required' with 'as is deemed necessary' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'shall require those persons to sign' with 'shall encourage those persons to sign' makes signing NDAs optional, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing the obligation to 'endeavor to restrict' and only 'encouraging' NDAs could be interpreted as not meeting the standard of reasonable efforts to protect confidential information, potentially leading to the loss of trade secret protection. The receiving party can argue they 'endeavored' but it wasn't enough, creating a loophole.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially violating CUTSA's requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The original text uses 'shall carefully restrict' and 'shall require,' creating a strong obligation. The changed text uses 'shall endeavor to restrict' and 'shall encourage,' which are weaker and create ambiguity, allowing the Receiving Party to argue they tried but failed. This undermines the 'duty to maintain secrecy' mentioned in California Civil Code 3426.1(a), as the encouragement of NDAs is not a strong enough measure to guarantee secrecy compared to a requirement to sign.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party should generally refrain from using Confidential Information for its own direct benefit, publishing, copying, transmitting, using, or disclosing it to others, especially if it could significantly harm the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'should generally refrain' makes the obligation less strict. Adding 'direct benefit' and 'significantly harm' introduces subjectivity and allows for loopholes where indirect benefits or minor harm might be argued as permissible.  The vagueness makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: While section 16600 generally prohibits restraints on trade, trade secrets are an exception. However, the modification introduces wiggle room, making the prohibition less clear, and thus potentially unenforceable. This means the receiving party might argue that their actions, while using confidential information, don't 'significantly harm' the disclosing party or that their benefit is not 'direct', therefore, they are not in violation. This ambiguity undermines the protection of trade secrets, acting as a restraint on trade if enforced strictly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation by replacing 'shall not' with 'should generally refrain,' introducing ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's responsibilities. Additionally, qualifiers like 'direct benefit' and 'significantly harm' create subjective standards, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to exploit loopholes. The Law Explanation highlights that this ambiguity undermines the protection of trade secrets and could be construed as a restraint on trade if strictly enforced. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly BPC 16600, states that contracts restraining trade are void, unless an exception applies. While trade secrets can be an exception, the added ambiguity weakens the trade secret protection, making the restraint on trade more likely to be voided, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party expressly agrees not to circumvent, avoid, bypass or obviate the Disclosing Party in any manner, directly or indirectly, to avoid payment of fees, royalties, or any other obligation in any way involving any of the parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, or any transaction contemplated hereunder or other business arrangement arising out of or connected in any way to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will use commercially reasonable efforts to avoid circumventing the Disclosing Party in any material way that directly results in a significant loss of revenue for the Disclosing Party related to parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'commercially reasonable efforts,' 'material way,' 'directly results,' and 'significant loss of revenue' makes the non-circumvention clause highly subjective and difficult to enforce. What constitutes 'commercially reasonable efforts' or a 'significant loss' is open to interpretation and legal challenge.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California contract law regarding good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicting a specific statute, the modified clause arguably violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in California contract law. By making the non-circumvention obligation so vague and conditional, it allows the Receiving Party to potentially undermine the very purpose of the agreement, depriving the Disclosing Party of the benefit of their bargain. The vague language provides loopholes that frustrate the intentions of the agreement and violate good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1304 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules [1301 - 1310] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 30. ) 1304. Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. (Added by renumbering Section 1203 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 19. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that undermine the original contract's intent. While the snippet (COM 1304) doesn't explicitly forbid subjective language, it mandates 'good faith' in contract performance. By adding 'commercially reasonable efforts,' 'material way,' 'directly results,' and 'significant loss of revenue,' the modified text creates loopholes. These subjective terms weaken the non-circumvention obligation, allowing the Receiving Party to potentially act in ways that, while technically not a direct circumvention, still undermine the Disclosing Party's expected benefits under the agreement. This arguably violates the implied covenant of good faith, as the modified clause makes the original obligation so vague it is rendered unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a business agreement, contract, or other business arrangement is entered into, each Party agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation, all Confidential Information in any form upon conclusion of the evaluation of entering into the transaction or other business arrangement or upon the specific request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will attempt to return or destroy, to the best of their ability, Confidential Information in a reasonable timeframe after the evaluation or upon request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation' to 'will attempt to return or destroy, to the best of their ability' significantly weakens the obligation. Adding 'in a reasonable timeframe' introduces ambiguity regarding the timing.  The lack of requirement for 'written confirmation' further weakens the clause, making it hard to prove compliance or breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 3426.1 addresses secrecy, 3426.2 discusses injunctions for misappropriation. The modified text's weakened obligation to return/destroy confidential information makes it more difficult to prove the threat of misappropriation, thus undermining the ability to obtain an injunction under CUTSA. If the confidential information is not effectively returned or destroyed (as the modified clause allows for some leeway), the risk of misappropriation remains high. The original language provides clear expectations for returning or destroying confidential information. If the terms of such return or destruction of that information is weakened, then confidential information is at greater risk of getting into the hands of parties that will use it to improperly compete. Therefore, the modified text weakens protections against misappropriation, undermining trade secrets protection under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The original text requires 'return or destroy, with written confirmation,' while the changed text states the party 'will attempt to return or destroy, to the best of their ability'. This change introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the obligation. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.2(a), states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined'. The weakened obligation to return or destroy confidential information increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially undermining the ability to obtain an injunction. The addition of 'in a reasonable timeframe' introduces further ambiguity regarding the timing of the return or destruction, thus the modification creates a contradiction.
